news_summary: Advancements in sustainable water technology, marine ecosystem protection, seed conservation efforts, and improved public transit in US cities highlight significant progress in environmental preservation and innovation.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées en matière de technologies durables liées à l'eau, de protection des écosystèmes marins, d'efforts de conservation des semences et d'amélioration des transports en commun dans les villes américaines témoignent des progrès significatifs réalisés dans les domaines de la préservation de l'environnement et de l'innovation.
news_summary_es: Los avances en tecnología hídrica sostenible, la protección de los ecosistemas marinos, las iniciativas de conservación de semillas y la mejora del transporte público en las ciudades estadounidenses ponen de manifiesto los importantes avances logrados en materia de preservación del medio ambiente e innovación.

articles.title: New solar desalination breakthrough makes fresh water without toxic brine
articles.summary: Scientists have developed a solar desalination system that turns seawater into drinking water without creating environmentally damaging brine. Special laser-textured metal panels use sunlight to evaporate water while automatically moving salt deposits away from the working surface, preventing clogging. The process was successfully tested with water from three oceans and can recover nearly all salts as solids. Those leftover materials could even become a source of valuable lithium for batteries.
articles.content:
According to the United Nations, 2.2 billion people still do not have access to safely managed drinking water. To help meet growing demand, many regions, from California to parts of the Middle East, rely on desalination plants that convert seawater into fresh water.

Traditional desalination methods such as reverse osmosis and thermal distillation can be expensive and energy intensive. They often require chemical treatments before and after processing the water and generate large volumes of concentrated saltwater known as brine. When discharged back into the ocean, brine can damage marine ecosystems by increasing salinity and reducing oxygen levels.

Researchers at the University of Rochester have developed a new approach that could address several of these challenges. Their solar powered desalination system produces fresh water efficiently, operates without chemical pretreatment, and avoids creating brine waste. The research was led by Chunlei Guo, a professor of optics and physics and a senior scientist at the University's Laboratory for Laser Energetics. The team described the technology in the journal Light: Science & Applications.

Laser-Treated Solar Panels Drive the Process

The system relies on specially engineered solar panels made from black metal that has been textured with femtosecond lasers. This treatment gives the surface two important properties. It absorbs nearly all incoming sunlight and strongly attracts water, a characteristic known as superwicking.

A laser patterned active region draws a thin layer of seawater across the panel. As sunlight is absorbed, the water evaporates and is distilled into fresh water. At the same time, dissolved salts and minerals are guided away from the active area and deposited onto untreated sections of the panel called passive regions.

By moving the salts away from the evaporation zone, the design prevents buildup that could otherwise interfere with continuous operation.

Using the Coffee Ring Effect to Prevent Clogging

Guo notes that several solar thermal desalination technologies have shown promising results in laboratory studies using simplified seawater composed only of water and sodium chloride.

In those experiments, sodium chloride crystals form in a loose, porous structure as water evaporates. Water can continue flowing through these crystals, dissolving them and making the systems relatively easy to clean.

Real seawater is far more complicated.

In addition to sodium chloride, oceans contain many other dissolved minerals. Materials containing magnesium and calcium often form hard, dense crusts when they crystallize. These deposits can block water flow and eventually shut down the desalination process.

The problem is similar to mineral scale building up inside a tea kettle or clogging a shower head over time, except seawater contains far higher concentrations of dissolved salts.

To overcome this challenge, the Rochester team carefully designed microscopic grooves on the black metal surface. The pattern encourages salts and minerals to move away from the active region before they can accumulate.

The researchers also took advantage of a familiar physical phenomenon known as the coffee ring effect.

"If you drop coffee on a surface, eventually the water evaporates and there's a ring left at the outer edge that is the concentrated coffee particles," says Guo. "We use that same principle to advance the salts to the passive region."

When the team tested the technology using water samples collected from the Pacific, Atlantic, and Indian Oceans, the surface effectively cleaned itself. Fresh water was continuously extracted while salts were directed toward the passive regions, where they could later be collected without reducing performance.

Recovering Valuable Minerals Instead of Creating Waste

One of the most significant advantages of the system is what happens to the leftover salt.

Conventional desalination produces liquid brine that must be treated, disposed of, or discharged into the environment. The new process instead recovers nearly all dissolved salts in solid form.

Those recovered materials could become valuable resources. In addition to producing table salt, the process could help extract important minerals such as lithium, a key ingredient in lithium ion batteries used in electric vehicles and many consumer electronics.

In a related study published in the Journal of Materials Chemistry A, Guo and colleagues demonstrated that the same superwicking solar panels can also separate lithium from other salts.

To accomplish this, the researchers embedded hydrogen titanate nanoparticles into the microscopic grooves of the black metal surface. These particles selectively isolate lithium from other dissolved minerals.

"Mining lithium from the Earth has proven to be very taxing from an energy and environmental standpoint, so pulling lithium directly from saltwater could be a very important future route," says Guo.

Using water from Utah's Great Salt Lake, the team successfully recovered about 50 percent of the lithium contained in the salts remaining after desalination.

Potential for Large Scale Fresh Water Production

Although the technology has so far been demonstrated only in proof of concept devices, Guo believes the approach can be scaled up significantly.

If successfully expanded, the system could help increase access to clean drinking water while also creating more sustainable sources of critical minerals.

The research was supported by the National Science Foundation, the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, and the Worldwide Universities Network. Additional contributors from the Institute of Optics included Senior Scientist Subash Singh, alumnus Ran Wei '24 (PhD), PhD students Luheng Tang and Tainshu Xu, and Mingjiang Ma.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant breakthrough in solar desalination technology that can produce fresh water without harmful brine waste, addressing a critical global water scarcity issue affecting billions. It also offers a sustainable method to recover valuable minerals like lithium, which has broad environmental and societal benefits. The story is well-detailed, focused on a single impactful innovation with potential for large-scale positive effects on water access and resource sustainability.
articles.personality_title: New solar desalination method creates fresh water without harmful brine
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many parts of the world need more fresh water, and desalination plants turn seawater into drinking water. But usual methods produce salty waste called brine, which can harm ocean life.

**What happened** – Scientists at the University of Rochester developed a solar-powered system that uses special laser-treated metal panels to evaporate seawater and collect fresh water. These panels push salt deposits away from the evaporation area, stopping clogging and avoiding harmful brine.

**Impact** – This system works with real seawater from the Pacific, Atlantic, and Indian Oceans, recovering almost all the salt as solid material instead of liquid waste. These solid salts can be collected and even used to extract valuable minerals like lithium, which is important for batteries.

**What's next step** – The researchers hope to scale up this technology to produce more fresh water and recover minerals on a larger scale, helping communities and industries while protecting the environment.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new solar desalination technology produces fresh water without toxic brine and recovers useful minerals from seawater.
articles.personality_title_fr: Nouvelle méthode solaire de dessalement produit de l’eau douce sans saumure nocive
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans de nombreuses régions du monde, il y a un besoin croissant d’eau douce. Les usines de dessalement transforment l’eau de mer en eau potable, mais elles produisent souvent une saumure salée qui peut nuire à la vie marine.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’Université de Rochester ont créé un système solaire utilisant des panneaux métalliques traités au laser pour évaporer l’eau de mer et recueillir de l’eau douce. Ces panneaux déplacent les dépôts de sel loin de la zone d’évaporation, évitant ainsi les blocages et la production de saumure nocive.

**Impact** – Ce système a été testé avec de l’eau des océans Pacifique, Atlantique et Indien, récupérant presque tout le sel sous forme solide au lieu de déchets liquides. Ces sels solides peuvent être collectés et utilisés pour extraire des minéraux précieux comme le lithium, essentiel pour les batteries.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs souhaitent développer cette technologie à plus grande échelle pour fournir plus d’eau douce et récupérer des minéraux, aidant ainsi les communautés et protégeant l’environnement.

**En résumé** – Une nouvelle technologie solaire de dessalement produit de l’eau douce sans saumure toxique et récupère des minéraux utiles de l’eau de mer.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método solar de desalinización crea agua dulce sin salmuera tóxica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas regiones del mundo necesitan más agua dulce, y las plantas de desalinización convierten el agua de mar en agua potable. Pero los métodos tradicionales generan salmuera que puede dañar la vida marina.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad de Rochester desarrollaron un sistema solar que usa paneles metálicos tratados con láser para evaporar el agua de mar y recolectar agua dulce. Estos paneles alejan los depósitos de sal de la zona de evaporación, evitando bloqueos y la creación de salmuera dañina.

**Impacto** – El sistema funcionó con agua de los océanos Pacífico, Atlántico e Índico, recuperando casi toda la sal en forma sólida en lugar de residuos líquidos. Estas sales sólidas pueden ser recolectadas y usadas para extraer minerales valiosos como el litio, importante para baterías.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren ampliar esta tecnología para producir más agua dulce y recuperar minerales a mayor escala, ayudando a comunidades y protegiendo el medio ambiente.

**Mensaje clave** – Una nueva tecnología solar de desalinización produce agua dulce sin salmuera tóxica y recupera minerales útiles del agua de mar.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a textured black solar panel with delicate microscopic grooves, glowing softly as it draws a thin, shimmering layer of seawater that evaporates into pure droplets rising upward, while crystalline salt formations gently migrate along patterned channels away from the evaporation zone, set against a serene ocean backdrop under warm sunlight, rendered in natural muted blues, blacks, and soft earth tones.

articles.title: The ocean's health may depend on a tiny microbe inside fish
articles.summary: A surprising new discovery suggests that tiny microbes living inside fish may be helping shape the chemistry of the world’s oceans. Scientists found evidence that bacteria in the guts of marine fish work alongside their hosts to produce calcium carbonate, a mineral that plays an important role in ocean health and carbon storage. For years, researchers believed fish handled this process on their own, but the new findings point to a hidden partnership between fish and microbes.
articles.content:
Scientists have uncovered evidence that tiny microbes living inside fish may be helping drive important processes that affect the world's oceans.

The research, led by former University of Miami graduate student Anthony Bonacolta, suggests that gut bacteria and marine fish work together to produce calcium carbonate, a mineral that plays a significant role in ocean chemistry and the marine carbon cycle. The findings point to a previously overlooked partnership that could influence how oceans store carbon and maintain overall health.

Researchers have long believed that this mineral production was controlled mainly by the fish themselves. The new study indicates that microbes living in the fish intestine may also be essential participants in the process.

Fish and Microbes Working Together

Bony fish, known as teleosts, constantly drink seawater to maintain proper hydration. As they process that seawater, excess calcium and carbonate ions are removed from the body and released as solid calcium carbonate pellets called ichthyocarbonates.

"This work suggests that the gut microbiome may play a broader role in both fish biology and global marine nutrient cycles," said one of the study's senior authors, Martin Grosell, Maytag Professor of Ichthyology and chair of the Department of Marine Biology and Ecology. "What was previously thought to be a process driven solely by the fish may actually reflect a close symbiosis between the fish and its gut microbial community."

Testing Fish in Different Salinity Conditions

To investigate the process, researchers conducted laboratory experiments using Gulf toadfish exposed to water with different salt concentrations. The fish were kept in brackish water (9 ppt), normal seawater (35 ppt), and hypersaline water (60 ppt).

Scientists wanted to determine how salinity affects ichthyocarbonate production, which is known to increase as fish adjust to saltier conditions through normal osmoregulation.

The results showed clear differences. Fish living in low-salinity water did not produce ichthyocarbonates. Fish kept in seawater did produce them, and production increased even further in the hypersaline environment.

Evidence From the Fish Gut Microbiome

The research team collected samples from multiple areas of the fish intestine, from the ichthyocarbonates themselves, and from the surrounding water.

DNA and RNA analyses allowed scientists to examine both the microbial communities living in the fish and patterns of gene activity in the fish and associated microbes. Genetic sequencing was used to identify the microorganisms present, while gene expression studies helped reveal potential biological functions connected to calcium carbonate formation.

The researchers found that vibrios, especially Photobacterium damselae subsp. damselae, were highly abundant in both the intestinal tract and the ichthyocarbonates. Genetic evidence suggested these bacteria possess capabilities associated with ichthyocarbonate production, indicating they may contribute directly to mineral formation alongside their fish hosts.

Implications for Ocean Health and the Carbon Cycle

The discovery highlights how microscopic organisms can influence large-scale environmental processes.

"Most life on Earth is microbial, driving nutrient cycles and ecosystem function while revealing new dimensions of biological diversity through symbiosis," said Grosell. "The ocean is especially rich in these partnerships, and the toadfish-vibrio symbiosis potentially linked to calcium carbonate production is a striking new example."

The findings provide new insight into the connections between marine animals, their microbiomes, and the global processes that help regulate ocean chemistry and carbon storage.

The research was supported by start-up funds from the University of Miami and by Project PID2023-152522NB-I00 financed by the Ministry of Science, Innovation, and Universities in Spain.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery revealing a symbiotic relationship between fish and gut microbes that contributes to calcium carbonate production, a key process affecting ocean health and carbon storage. This finding has broad implications for understanding and potentially protecting marine ecosystems and global carbon cycles, representing a meaningful environmental breakthrough with clear context and impact.
articles.personality_title: Tiny microbes inside fish help shape ocean chemistry
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have studied how fish produce calcium carbonate, a mineral important for ocean health and carbon storage. Until now, it was believed that fish did this process alone.

**What happened** – Researchers discovered that bacteria living in the guts of marine fish work together with the fish to produce calcium carbonate. Experiments with Gulf toadfish showed that fish in saltier water produce more of these calcium carbonate pellets, called ichthyocarbonates. Genetic tests revealed that certain bacteria, especially a type called Photobacterium damselae, are common inside the fish and may help form these minerals.

**Impact** – This finding reveals a new partnership between fish and microbes that affects how oceans store carbon and maintain their chemistry. It changes what scientists thought about fish biology and shows how tiny organisms inside animals can influence big environmental processes.

**What’s next step** – Scientists will likely study this fish-microbe relationship more closely and explore how it affects different fish species and ocean environments. This could improve our understanding of marine ecosystems and help protect ocean health in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Tiny microbes living inside fish help produce minerals that play a key role in ocean chemistry and carbon storage.
articles.personality_title_fr: De minuscules microbes dans les poissons influencent la chimie des océans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudiaient comment les poissons produisent du carbonate de calcium, un minéral important pour la santé des océans et le stockage du carbone. Jusqu’à présent, on pensait que les poissons faisaient ce processus seuls.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont découvert que des bactéries vivant dans les intestins des poissons marins travaillent avec eux pour produire du carbonate de calcium. Des expériences avec des poissons crapauds du Golfe ont montré que les poissons dans une eau plus salée produisent plus de ces pellets de carbonate de calcium, appelés ichthyocarbonates. Des tests génétiques ont révélé que certaines bactéries, notamment Photobacterium damselae, sont fréquentes dans les poissons et pourraient aider à former ces minéraux.

**Impact** – Cette découverte révèle un nouveau partenariat entre les poissons et les microbes qui influence la manière dont les océans stockent le carbone et maintiennent leur chimie. Cela change la vision de la biologie des poissons et montre comment de minuscules organismes peuvent affecter des processus environnementaux importants.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont probablement étudier plus en détail cette relation entre poissons et microbes, et voir comment elle affecte différentes espèces et environnements marins. Cela pourrait améliorer notre compréhension des écosystèmes marins et aider à protéger la santé des océans.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – De minuscules microbes vivant dans les poissons aident à produire des minéraux essentiels à la chimie et au stockage du carbone dans les océans.
articles.personality_title_es: Microbios diminutos dentro de los peces ayudan a formar la química del océano
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudiaban cómo los peces producen carbonato de calcio, un mineral importante para la salud del océano y el almacenamiento de carbono. Hasta ahora, se creía que los peces hacían este proceso por sí solos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores descubrieron que bacterias que viven en los intestinos de peces marinos trabajan junto con ellos para producir carbonato de calcio. Experimentos con peces sapo del Golfo mostraron que los peces en agua más salada producen más de estos pellets de carbonato de calcio llamados ictiocarbonatos. Pruebas genéticas revelaron que ciertas bacterias, especialmente Photobacterium damselae, son comunes dentro de los peces y pueden ayudar a formar estos minerales.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo muestra una nueva asociación entre peces y microbios que afecta cómo los océanos almacenan carbono y mantienen su química. Cambia la forma en que los científicos entienden la biología de los peces y muestra cómo organismos diminutos dentro de animales pueden influir en grandes procesos ambientales.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos probablemente estudiarán más esta relación entre peces y microbios y explorarán cómo afecta a diferentes especies y ambientes oceánicos. Esto podría mejorar el conocimiento sobre los ecosistemas marinos y ayudar a proteger la salud del océano.

**Resumen en una frase** – Microbios diminutos que viven dentro de los peces ayudan a producir minerales clave para la química y el almacenamiento de carbono en los océanos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a graceful Gulf toadfish swimming gently in softly textured ocean water, its translucent gut subtly glowing to reveal swirling clusters of friendly, luminous Vibrio microbes forming delicate, sparkling calcium carbonate pellets around it, symbolizing their vital partnership sustaining ocean health and the marine carbon cycle.

articles.title: The seed guardian of Brazil’s Caatinga
articles.summary: In Minas Gerais, Brazil, a catingueiro shares his home with the memory of his ancestors and generations of plant varieties.
articles.content:
This article was published on January 29, 2026, by Tatiana Merlino from O Joio e O Trigo, an independent Brazilian news outlet. An excerpt of this piece is being republished on Global Voices under a content partnership agreement. It is part of Global Voices’ May 2026 Spotlight series, “Global crisis, local solutions.” You can support this coverage by donating here.

Standing up, hat on his head, glasses on his face, and accordion in his hand, Geraldo Gomes sings one of his original songs: “I live in the middle of the woods, close to Mother Nature, enjoying the fragrance of the flowers… My field is diverse, I plant a little of several varieties…” As he plays, his mother, Dona Rita, dances.

We are in the family’s living room, in the community of Touro, in the town of Serranópolis de Minas, in the northern part of the state of Minas Gerais. On the walls hang family photos, diplomas, and certificates from the countless courses Geraldo has taken.

There is also a table covered with jars filled with seeds and bottles with liqueurs he makes using plants grown in his fields. “The liqueur, I make from several plants. From pumpkin, banana, corn, juá de boi, to moringa. There must be about thirty types we make here.”

Geraldo Gomes is 62 years old and was born in this house, the same place where his mother was born and where his grandmother was married. The son and grandson of farmers, he began going to the fields at seven years old, accompanying his father and grandfather, who always maintained that “a field should be like the forest, it has to have many types of plants.” They planted everything: rice, peanuts, sugarcane, pumpkin, maxixe, corn, beans, broad beans, and more.

One of the family’s practices, part of the sertanejo people’s tradition passed down through generations and kept alive by Geraldo, is saving and exchanging seeds. “We saved them so we would not be dependent. Sometimes, when it was time to buy, you could not find them, and many times, when you did find them, you did not have the money.”

If the farmer’s living room draws attention for its richness of detail, his seed house is even more striking. Shelves hold jars and plastic bottles filled with hundreds of seeds in many colors and sizes. Across the floor, even more bottles. And dozens of gourds. He says he has more than 200 varieties of seeds.

One hundred years of preservation

Geraldo is known and celebrated as an important guardian of heirloom seeds in his region. “A guardian is someone who has several species of many plants, preserves them, and seeks out other varieties that are disappearing. He saves and multiplies them. We have species of seeds that have been planted for more than 100 years. We keep them to show the importance they have had, and the importance they may have for future generations,” he explains. “Today we see that most of these species are disappearing, whether native or cultivated.”

The family farm is located in the semi-arid Caatinga, where the climate is marked by long periods of drought, food is produced without pesticides, and the seeds are selected and stored. There are seeds for watermelon, pumpkin, cotton, and more than 70 types of beans: white, black, yellow, red, striped, artisanal, and more. There are also seeds for broad beans, okra, maxixe, castor beans, and peanuts. He also keeps seeds of medicinal plants. As for corn, there are countless varieties: white corn, used to make canjica; coruja corn, colorful and fluffy, with a large ear that is good for animals; black crioulo corn; Tupiniquim corn; catingueiro corn; cateto corn.

At the entrance to the seed house, a sign indicates that the initiative is supported by the One Land and Two Waters Program (Programa Uma Terra e Duas Águas) of the Semi-arid Network (Articulação do Semiárido), a coalition that advocates for living harmoniously with nature in the semi-arid region. The Center for Alternative Agriculture of Northern Minas, which promotes agroecology and the rights of traditional peoples and communities in the region, is also one of the organizations supporting Gomes’ work.

Climate change

The role of seed guardian began very early in Gomes’ life. At the beginning, there was no dedicated house for the seeds; they were stored in a corn crib. “And every passing year, we have been selecting these seeds. Today, what you find most often on the market are transgenic species,” he laments. He sells his seeds at open-air markets, at seed exchange fairs, or by order.

His preservation work faces many difficulties. “If you do not have a lot of patience and love for the seeds, you end up giving up. There are few incentives and few people who are interested in this work. And I look at the benefits you can gain from what you plant. It is a guarantee in the face of these difficulties, when there are so many pesticides. Because the entire planting system has changed.”

Geraldo maintains an agroecological field where he adopts an agroforestry system, or SAF. Walking through his property, he shows us fruit trees — such as tamarind, sugar apple, cashew, and jambolan — and medicinal plants. “It is a way of having everything at the same time: an integrated agroforest for food production and soil fertilization, in addition to preserving the environment.”

The diversity of plants and products is undeniable, but the arrival of monoculture farms nearby and the effects of climate change harm production. “When you do not use pesticides but the other person does — when he is spraying, you can smell it in the air. So it harms us. And today they are even using drones to spray pesticides.” The river water is also contaminated, he reports. “We see this with sadness, because before the monoculture of cotton, the river flowed. You might have had little rain, but it still flowed. With the destruction that happened came deforestation. Today, everything is being lost, and climate change is because of exactly that.”

In the 1970s, livestock, irrigation, and cotton monoculture projects arrived in the region, using agricultural practices promoted by the so-called Green Revolution: hybrid seeds, chemical fertilizers, pesticides, and mechanization. According to Gomes, “it was a period of great destruction… Many native plants were cleared for cotton planting. And people went through a lot of hardship. Because the cotton monoculture was financed by the bank, and people became financially dependent on them/the system. As a result, many lost their land to pay their debts,” he recalls. “There were times when you were in the river, you went to bathe in the afternoon, and you would see that little stream of poison coming down, kind of green, like grease.”

“And this system that I plant here — many times people would say it was a crazy person’s field, that I must be crazy to use this system,” he says. “But at least I preserved the life of many species. It was because of this craziness. Otherwise, everything might be gone.”



Geraldo’s “crazy field” is essential for preserving the Caatinga. Between 1985 and 2023, the biome lost 14.4 percent, or 8.6 million hectares, of its native vegetation cover, according to the MapBiomas platform. What remains represents 59.6 percent of the biome, or 51.4 million hectares. In almost four decades, the area dedicated to agriculture expanded by 1.8 million hectares. There were 115,000 hectares occupied by the activity in 1985, and in 2023, it reached 1.9 million hectares. Pastures increased the most: 12.1 million hectares, a growth of 112 percent between 1985 and 2023, rising from 10.8 million to 22.9 million hectares.

Geraldo says he wants to transform the seed house into a seed museum. “Whoever comes always takes the liqueurs. And we reinforce the importance of preserving the importance of family farming.”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the inspiring real-world impact of Geraldo Gomes' dedicated preservation of heirloom seeds and agroecological farming in Brazil's Caatinga biome, which contributes to biodiversity conservation, sustainable agriculture, and climate resilience. The story has broad significance by addressing environmental preservation and food sovereignty in a semi-arid region facing climate change and monoculture threats. It provides substantial context about the cultural heritage, ecological importance, and challenges faced, making it a meaningful and uplifting environmental success story.
articles.personality_title: Geraldo Gomes preserves rare seeds to protect Brazil’s Caatinga plants
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In Brazil’s semi-arid Caatinga region, farming faces challenges like drought, climate change, and the spread of large farms that use pesticides and monoculture. Many native plants and traditional seeds are disappearing.

**What happened** – Geraldo Gomes, a 62-year-old farmer from Minas Gerais, has saved and grown more than 200 varieties of seeds passed down through his family for over 100 years. He stores them in a special seed house and sells or exchanges them to keep these plants alive. He also practices agroecology by growing many types of plants and trees together without pesticides.

**Impact** – Geraldo’s work helps protect plant diversity and traditional farming methods in a region where many native plants have been lost. His seed collection is important because it preserves plants that can survive drought and don’t need chemicals, offering a natural way to farm sustainably. This also helps local families remain independent from big seed companies.

**What’s next step** – Geraldo wants to turn his seed house into a museum to teach others about the value of family farming and seed preservation. His efforts are supported by local organizations that promote living in balance with nature in the semi-arid region.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By saving and sharing heirloom seeds, Geraldo Gomes protects Brazil’s plant heritage and supports farming that works with nature in a tough environment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Geraldo Gomes préserve des graines rares pour protéger les plantes de la Caatinga au Brésil
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans la région semi-aride de la Caatinga au Brésil, l’agriculture est confrontée à des défis comme la sécheresse, le changement climatique et la multiplication des grandes exploitations utilisant pesticides et monoculture. De nombreuses plantes natives et graines traditionnelles disparaissent.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Geraldo Gomes, agriculteur de 62 ans dans le Minas Gerais, a conservé et cultivé plus de 200 variétés de graines transmises dans sa famille depuis plus de 100 ans. Il les garde dans une maison dédiée aux graines et les vend ou échange pour préserver ces plantes. Il pratique aussi l’agroécologie en cultivant plusieurs types de plantes et d’arbres ensemble sans pesticides.

**Impact** – Le travail de Geraldo aide à protéger la diversité des plantes et les méthodes agricoles traditionnelles dans une région où beaucoup de plantes natives ont disparu. Sa collection de graines est importante car elle conserve des plantes résistantes à la sécheresse et sans besoin de produits chimiques, offrant une agriculture durable et naturelle. Cela aide aussi les familles locales à rester indépendantes des grandes entreprises semencières.

**Prochaine étape** – Geraldo souhaite transformer sa maison de graines en musée pour enseigner aux autres la valeur de l’agriculture familiale et de la conservation des graines. Ses efforts sont soutenus par des organisations locales qui encouragent une vie en harmonie avec la nature dans cette région semi-aride.

**Résumé en une phrase** – En sauvegardant et partageant des graines anciennes, Geraldo Gomes protège le patrimoine végétal du Brésil et soutient une agriculture en harmonie avec la nature dans un environnement difficile.
articles.personality_title_es: Geraldo Gomes conserva semillas raras para proteger las plantas de la Caatinga en Brasil
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En la región semiárida de la Caatinga en Brasil, la agricultura enfrenta desafíos como la sequía, el cambio climático y la expansión de grandes fincas que usan pesticidas y monocultivos. Muchas plantas nativas y semillas tradicionales están desapareciendo.

**Qué pasó** – Geraldo Gomes, un agricultor de 62 años en Minas Gerais, ha salvado y cultivado más de 200 variedades de semillas que su familia ha conservado por más de 100 años. Las guarda en una casa especial para semillas y las vende o intercambia para mantener vivas estas plantas. También practica la agroecología cultivando varios tipos de plantas y árboles juntos sin usar pesticidas.

**Impacto** – El trabajo de Geraldo ayuda a proteger la diversidad de plantas y los métodos agrícolas tradicionales en una región donde muchas plantas nativas se han perdido. Su colección de semillas es importante porque conserva plantas resistentes a la sequía y que no necesitan químicos, ofreciendo una forma natural y sostenible de cultivar. Esto también ayuda a las familias locales a no depender de las grandes compañías de semillas.

**Próximo paso** – Geraldo quiere convertir su casa de semillas en un museo para enseñar a otros sobre el valor de la agricultura familiar y la conservación de semillas. Su trabajo cuenta con el apoyo de organizaciones locales que promueven vivir en equilibrio con la naturaleza en la región semiárida.

**Frase resumen** – Al salvar y compartir semillas tradicionales, Geraldo Gomes protege el patrimonio vegetal de Brasil y apoya una agricultura que trabaja con la naturaleza en un entorno difícil.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a rustic Brazilian seed house filled with colorful jars and bottles of diverse heirloom seeds and homemade liqueurs, surrounded by symbolic representations of a vibrant agroforestry field with fruit trees like cashew and sugar apple, and an abstract flowing river subtly showing signs of environmental strain, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and gentle light.

articles.title: US host cities made transit improvements a World Cup goooooooal
articles.content:
The latest addition to Seattle’s already impressive public transit system opened to great fanfare this spring when more than 200,000 people rode the Crosslake Connection light rail line.

Its March 28 debut was second only to the parade that followed the Seahawks’ Super Bowl victory as Sound Transit’s busiest day ever. Trains now glide across Lake Washington on what is believed to be the world’s first electric rail line that spans a floating bridge, linking the city with Bellevue and Redmond, and doubling the frequency of stops in the heart of Emerald City.

Those same tracks will carry tens of thousands of fans downtown to Lumen Field for the six World Cup matches the city will host between June 15 and July 6. Kirk Hovenkotter, who leads the transit advocacy organization Transportation Choices Coalition, has no doubt that Seattle’s sustained commitment to public transit helped it become a host city.

This summer’s spotlight follows an earlier snub. When the World Cup came to the United States in 1994, Seattle hoped to host matches at Husky Stadium but came away empty-handed.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

In the 32 years since, the metropolitan area has grown from 2.5 million people to more than four million. Its transportation infrastructure has boomed as well. Steady investment that began with voter approval of the Sound Move transit package in 1996 helped launch light rail in 2008 and turn Seattle into one of the country’s most ambitious builders of public transit. This summer’s World Cup became the deadline for opening the Crosslake Connection.

“Our region hasn’t been preparing for the World Cup for 18 months,” Hovenkotter said. “It’s been preparing for 18 years.”

Seattle is one of 16 cities, 11 of them in the U.S., that will host matches in a tournament FIFA, the sports’ sanctioning body, expects to draw more than five million fans. Several are using the event as an opportunity to open rail lines, redesign bus networks, and make other changes that will benefit residents long after the final match. Some cities used the tournament as a deadline. In others, it helped build support for projects or push delayed efforts over the goal line.

These investments come as rail and bus systems nationwide continue recovering from the steep ridership decline sparked by the pandemic while confronting aging infrastructure and a dire financial outlook. In a country that is less supportive of mass transit than other nations, the World Cup has become an unusual catalyst for change.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

Plenty of stadiums remain disconnected from public transportation, of course. But what’s happening in places like Seattle and Atlanta shows that a mega-event like the World Cup can strengthen transit systems — if the investment starts long before kick-off.

The World Cup’s infrastructure legacy has often been more cautionary than celebratory. Past tournaments have raised questions not only about human rights violations and environmental harm, but about whether host cities deliver the public benefits they promise. Brazil and South Africa, for example, failed to fulfill the mass transit commitments they made.

Such disappointments often reflect a broader problem: Host cities plan first for the event, then for the people who live there, said Simon Kuper, who wrote World Cup Fever and has attended nine World Cups. He likens it to hosting a wedding. “Let’s say it’s at the house,” he said. “You paint the house, you fix the toilet, you fix the door that wasn’t working, you redo the kitchen.”

But the transit needs of 80,000 fans differ from those of residents. “You risk overinvesting in the route to the stadium and not in what makes residents’ lives better every day.”

Seattle followed a different plan. The $1 billion Crosslake Connection was not built for the World Cup –– the money came from a funding package voters approved in 2008, 14 years before Seattle’s selection as host city –– but Sound Transit used it as a deadline for finishing a project that was three years behind schedule.

“It was like, ‘We’re going to do everything. We’re going to move heaven and earth. We’re going to be working every shift to make sure that when the world is here, our flagship bridge and our double capacity are ready to run passengers,’ and they were,” said Henry Bendon, a public information officer with the agency.

The $1 billion Crosslake Connection was not built for the World Cup, but Sound Transit used it as a deadline for finishing a project that was three years behind schedule. Courtesy of Sound Transit

Building infrastructure matters, but so does helping people use it. Brian McCullough, who lived in Seattle from 2014 until 2020 and is now an associate professor of sport management at the University of Michigan, said communication will be key to the system’s success.

Here, too, Seattle has a blueprint. When it hosted the 2018 Special Olympics USA Games, McCullough helped with a campaign encouraging athletes, coaches, and caretakers to use alternative transportation. The plan included providing them with free rides on the city’s expansive light rail system. It worked: Initially, 78 percent of participants planned to rent a car, but in the end, only 7 percent did. Sound Transit has an extensive messaging campaign geared toward soccer fans, including signage in the languages of the countries playing in Seattle.

That lesson is shaping preparations for the World Cup that could further benefit residents, too. Sound Transit expanded its airport bus service to provide 24-hour rides to and from Seattle. The Legislature funded an intercity bus between Pasco, a city in the state’s rapidly growing southwestern corner that is hosting a tournament event, and Spokane, which is hosting an Egyptian team with one of the sport’s biggest stars. It also increased frequency on other routes throughout the state. Hovenkotter hopes those improvements are here to stay.

“It’s going to be hard to disinvest in this once these start running and people start benefiting from it,” he said.

Some 2,600 miles to the southeast, another city is preparing for an influx of soccer fans. The Metropolitan Atlanta Rapid Transit Authority, or MARTA, is rolling out a major redesign of its bus network and preparing new railcars with expanded capacity, moves that will move more people more often during the event — and long after it.

Like Seattle, Atlanta did not make the list of 1994 World Cup host cities. But two years later, it faced a bigger transportation challenge: the 1996 Summer Olympics. MARTA added 7 miles of rail to ensure everyone got around efficiently.

Today, the system, which typically carries more than 5 million passengers per month, has 48 miles of track and more than 1,500 miles of bus network.

Soccer fans will discover a system overhauled first and foremost to serve residents. Beginning in 2021, MARTA started working with the community on the first revamp in 40 years. The remake launched in April, and although it cut the number of bus lines from 113 to 81, the agency said the change increased the number of residents who live within a quarter mile of a stop. It also nearly tripled the number of residents living near a route with buses that arrive every 15 minutes, according to MARTA.

MARTA also added a rapid transit line in downtown Atlanta and introduced 12 on-demand “microtransit zones” in which vans provide short rides within each zone.

Among other things, Metropolitan Atlanta Rapid Transit Authority overhauled its bus system in a makeover that nearly tripled the number of residents living near a route with buses that arrive every 15 minutes. Metropolitan Atlanta Rapid Transit Authority

The rail system saw similar changes. MARTA plans to update all 224 train cars, some of which have been in service since the 1980s, with more spacious interiors starting in June. Each four-car train will carry 752 passengers, a 13 percent increase. That will be a boon during the tournament, given that four stations are within walking distance of Mercedes-Benz Stadium.

The World Cup provided an incentive to move quickly. “Folks around here figured out if I want to get my projects some priority … I need to say ‘I want to do this for the World Cup,’” said Rhonda Allen, the agency’s deputy general manager.

Not everyone is convinced these projects will benefit the community, however. Bakari Height, co-founder of the transit advocacy group MARTA Army, said transit has stagnated since the Olympics, with only two stations added. He called the new trains a “subtle upgrade” and the bus redesign a “sour point” because it cut routes. He doubts the system will handle the World Cup.

“I don’t know if they’re really ready,” he said, “and for sure, not ready for these crowds.”

In some cities, the changes are smaller, but still practical.

The Massachusetts Bay Transit Agency will open an expanded station near Gillette Stadium in Foxboro this month. The $35 million project adds an additional platform that improves accessibility and allows the station to handle more cars. Caitlin Allen-Connelly, executive director of the advocacy group Transit Matters, said the upgrades will benefit people headed to New England Patriots games and concerts long after the tournament ends. “There was definitely a need to make beautification and accessibility standards to be able to accommodate this level of service for the World Cup,” she said.

That said, moving all those soccer fans around will impact residents. The MBTA is also reducing commuter rail service on most lines during the tournament. The transit agency said it has “made some minor reductions and adjustments” to service on non-game days to account for the need to reconfigure trains and make other changes to suit the influx of riders to the stadium to watch matches.

Kansas City Streetcar extended its southern service by 3.5 miles last fall and opened a 0.7-mile northern extension in May. While the line does not reach Arrowhead Stadium, it will help soccer fans reach the “Fan Fest” events that accompany matches. Shuttle buses will carry fans from there to the stadium. Tom Gerend, executive director of the Kansas City Streetcar Authority, said the city highlighted the growing system in its host-city bid and that the tournament provided additional pressure to finish projects. “We’re certainly using the World Cup as motivation to make progress and to have these services up and running in time,” he said.

Whether transit projects for the World Cup provide lasting gains often depends on who pays for them — and whether cities keep investing after the tournament ends.

So far, the federal government has done little to help host cities with this. The Department of Transportation allocated $100 million in March, or roughly $10 million per city — far too little to transform most transit systems. FIFA does not contribute anything toward transportation costs. That’s forced cities to seek funding elsewhere, including the fare box. The Massachusetts Bay Transportation Authority plans to charge $80 for round-trip train tickets to each World Cup match in Boston, while NJ Transit will charge $105 for round-trip tickets to games in New York.

Balsam Nehme, director of sustainability at Sidara Collaborative, a firm that advises on large-scale infrastructure and sustainability projects, said the World Cup can bolster greener transit if cities use it to test new ideas and accelerate existing plans. That can mean short-term fixes like shuttle buses or long-term investments like light rail, she said, so long as they fit broader sustainability goals. The priority, she said, should be “long-term system-level thinking.”

For Gerend, the most important question was what would be useful after the fans left. Kansas City, he said, avoided spending big on permanent event services with little long-term value. That meant using the World Cup as a deadline, not a blueprint. “Let’s invest our resources in permanent solutions that are part of a long-standing, regional plan that will have staying power.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports on significant, long-term public transit infrastructure improvements in multiple US cities catalyzed by the World Cup event. These investments enhance sustainable transportation options, reduce reliance on cars, and benefit millions of residents beyond the tournament. The story provides detailed context on projects, funding, and community impact, highlighting a meaningful positive environmental and societal change.
articles.personality_title: World Cup sparks lasting transit upgrades in US host cities
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Several US cities hosting the 2026 World Cup have made major improvements to their public transportation systems. These upgrades build on years of planning and investment, aiming to handle large crowds during the event and serve residents long after the tournament.

**What happened** – Seattle opened its new Crosslake Connection light rail line in March, linking the city with nearby suburbs and doubling train frequency downtown. This line, built years before the World Cup was awarded, became a key route for fans attending matches at Lumen Field. Similarly, Atlanta redesigned its bus network and expanded rail capacity to move more people efficiently. Other cities like Boston, Kansas City, and those in Washington state also expanded or improved transit stations and routes ahead of the games.

**Impact** – These transit upgrades go beyond just the World Cup. Seattle’s $1 billion rail project was funded by voters years ago but used the tournament as a deadline to finish. The changes help reduce car traffic and make daily travel easier for millions. Atlanta’s bus redesign nearly tripled residents’ access to frequent service. Even smaller projects, like station expansions in Boston, improve accessibility for future events and everyday riders. This shows how a big sports event can encourage cities to invest in long-term transportation solutions.

**What's next step** – Cities will focus on keeping these transit improvements running and useful after the World Cup ends. Communication campaigns, like Seattle’s multilingual signage and free rides during past events, help people use the new systems. Some places face challenges, like funding limits or concerns about whether changes meet community needs. But officials plan to continue expanding and maintaining transit to support growth and sustainability.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The 2026 World Cup has accelerated important public transit projects in US host cities, creating lasting benefits for millions beyond the tournament.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Coupe du Monde stimule des améliorations durables des transports en commun dans les villes hôtes américaines
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Plusieurs villes américaines accueillant la Coupe du Monde 2026 ont réalisé d’importantes améliorations de leurs transports en commun. Ces travaux s’appuient sur des années de planification et d’investissements, visant à gérer les grandes foules pendant l’événement et à servir les habitants bien après le tournoi.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Seattle a inauguré en mars sa nouvelle ligne de métro léger Crosslake Connection, reliant la ville aux banlieues proches et doublant la fréquence des trains au centre-ville. Cette ligne, construite des années avant l’attribution de la Coupe du Monde, est devenue une route clé pour les fans assistant aux matchs au Lumen Field. De même, Atlanta a repensé son réseau de bus et augmenté la capacité de ses trains pour déplacer plus de personnes efficacement. D’autres villes comme Boston, Kansas City et dans l’État de Washington ont aussi élargi ou amélioré leurs gares et trajets avant les matchs.

**Impact** – Ces améliorations des transports vont bien au-delà de la Coupe du Monde. Le projet ferroviaire à 1 milliard de dollars de Seattle a été financé par les électeurs des années auparavant, mais le tournoi a servi de date limite pour son achèvement. Ces changements aident à réduire la circulation automobile et facilitent les déplacements quotidiens de millions de personnes. La refonte des bus à Atlanta a presque triplé l’accès des habitants à un service fréquent. Même les projets plus petits, comme l’agrandissement des gares à Boston, améliorent l’accessibilité pour les futurs événements et les usagers quotidiens. Cela montre comment un grand événement sportif peut encourager les villes à investir dans des solutions de transport durables à long terme.

**Prochaine étape** – Les villes vont se concentrer sur le maintien de ces améliorations après la Coupe du Monde. Des campagnes de communication, comme la signalisation multilingue à Seattle et la gratuité des trajets lors d’événements précédents, aident les gens à utiliser ces nouveaux systèmes. Certaines villes font face à des défis, comme des limites de financement ou des questions sur l’adéquation des changements aux besoins des communautés. Mais les responsables prévoient de continuer à développer et entretenir les transports pour soutenir la croissance et la durabilité.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – La Coupe du Monde 2026 a accéléré des projets importants de transports en commun dans les villes hôtes américaines, créant des bénéfices durables pour des millions de personnes au-delà du tournoi.
articles.personality_title_es: La Copa Mundial impulsa mejoras duraderas en el transporte público de ciudades anfitrionas de EE. UU.
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Varias ciudades de Estados Unidos que serán sede de la Copa Mundial 2026 han realizado importantes mejoras en sus sistemas de transporte público. Estas mejoras se basan en años de planificación e inversión, con el objetivo de manejar grandes multitudes durante el evento y servir a los residentes mucho después del torneo.

**Qué pasó** – Seattle inauguró en marzo su nueva línea de tren ligero Crosslake Connection, que conecta la ciudad con suburbios cercanos y duplica la frecuencia de trenes en el centro. Esta línea, construida años antes de que se asignara la Copa Mundial, se convirtió en una ruta clave para los aficionados que asisten a los partidos en Lumen Field. De manera similar, Atlanta rediseñó su red de autobuses y amplió la capacidad del tren para mover a más personas de forma eficiente. Otras ciudades como Boston, Kansas City y algunas en el estado de Washington también ampliaron o mejoraron estaciones y rutas antes de los partidos.

**Impacto** – Estas mejoras en el transporte van más allá de la Copa Mundial. El proyecto ferroviario de 1.000 millones de dólares en Seattle fue financiado por votantes años antes, pero el torneo sirvió como fecha límite para completarlo. Los cambios ayudan a reducir el tráfico de autos y facilitan el traslado diario de millones de personas. La remodelación de autobuses en Atlanta casi triplicó el acceso de los residentes a un servicio frecuente. Incluso los proyectos más pequeños, como la expansión de estaciones en Boston, mejoran la accesibilidad para futuros eventos y usuarios diarios. Esto muestra cómo un gran evento deportivo puede motivar a las ciudades a invertir en soluciones de transporte sostenibles a largo plazo.

**Próximo paso** – Las ciudades se enfocarán en mantener estas mejoras después de que termine la Copa Mundial. Campañas de comunicación, como señalización multilingüe en Seattle y viajes gratis durante eventos anteriores, ayudan a la gente a usar los nuevos sistemas. Algunas ciudades enfrentan desafíos, como limitaciones de financiamiento o dudas sobre si los cambios satisfacen las necesidades de la comunidad. Pero los funcionarios planean seguir expandiendo y manteniendo el transporte para apoyar el crecimiento y la sostenibilidad.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La Copa Mundial 2026 ha acelerado importantes proyectos de transporte público en ciudades anfitrionas de EE. UU., creando beneficios duraderos para millones más allá del torneo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a sleek electric light rail train gracefully crossing a floating bridge over a shimmering lake, with stylized silhouettes of cheering crowds and soccer balls subtly integrated into the flowing water beneath, symbolizing the harmony of advanced public transit and the vibrant World Cup energy uniting Seattle and its neighboring cities.
